question: A packaged product bundles a whole life policy with a critical illness rider the buyer cannot omit. Two years in, the client asks to drop only the CI rider while keeping the base cover. The insurer:
options: May decline, because in a packaged product the rider is folded in and not separately removable
Must remove the rider on request since riders are always optional
Must refund the rider premiums paid to date
Must convert the rider into an additional benefit type
explanation: Riders can usually be added or removed, but where a product is sold as a package with the rider folded in, the buyer cannot pick it out. That is the stated exception to the 'riders can be removed' rule.

question: A whole life policy lapses. On reinstatement, the insurer restores the base cover but declines the attached CI rider. This is:
options: Not permitted; the rider must follow the base policy automatically
Permitted; on reinstatement the insurer may refuse to bring the rider back
Permitted only if the rider had no cash value
Permitted only if premiums were unpaid for over six months
explanation: Reinstating the base policy does not drag the rider back. The insurer may reinstate the base yet refuse the rider, regardless of cash value or lapse length.

question: A client becomes totally and permanently disabled but is still able to earn a small income tutoring from home. Under the common TPD definition in the Waiver rider, does he meet it?
options: Yes, because he cannot do his previous job
Yes, because any disability qualifies
No, because he can still do some work to earn wages
No, because tutoring is not a recognised occupation
explanation: The common TPD definition requires that there be NO work, occupation or profession he can EVER do to earn any wages. Being able to earn from tutoring means he does not meet it. 'Any work, ever', not just his own job.

question: A client's Waiver of Premium rider has a six-month waiting period. He is disabled and dies in month four, still paying premiums. The main purpose the waiting period was serving here was to:
options: Delay the death claim by four months
Increase the cash value before any waiver
Allow the insurer to re-underwrite the policy
Confirm the disability was permanent before waiving premiums
explanation: The waiting period exists to confirm the disability genuinely meets the permanent definition before premiums are waived. Permanence takes time to prove, which is why it is longer than the CI diagnosis wait.

question: Why is the Waiver/TPD waiting period (six months) longer than the Critical Illness rider waiting period (30-90 days)?
options: Permanence of disability takes longer to confirm than a diagnosis
TPD claims are larger in value
Regulations require a fixed six-month gap for all riders
CI claims are paid in instalments
explanation: A diagnosis is relatively quick to confirm; proving a disability is PERMANENT needs a longer observation window. The length of each waiting period tracks how long its trigger takes to prove.

question: A client holds S$1.4m TPD cover on one policy and S$900,000 on a second policy, both with the same insurer. He suffers TPD. Assuming a typical S$2m aggregate cap, the insurer pays approximately:
options: S$2,300,000 (the full total of both)
S$2,000,000 (capped at the aggregate limit)
S$1,400,000 (the larger policy only)
S$900,000 (the smaller policy only)
explanation: The aggregate cap applies across all policies with that insurer. S$1.4m + S$0.9m = S$2.3m, but the payout is capped at S$2m. Splitting across policies does not beat the cap.

question: A client's policy provides a 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness. His adviser suggests also adding a Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider. The best assessment is:
options: A sensible addition that protects future premiums
Required by MAS for all CI policies
Pointless, because a 100% acceleration claim terminates the policy, leaving no premiums to waive
Only useful if the policy is an endowment
explanation: With 100% acceleration, a CI claim pays the full sum assured and ends the policy. There are then no future premiums to waive, so the waiver rider can never fire.

question: A S$400,000 whole life policy carries a 25% Acceleration Benefit CI rider. The client is diagnosed with a covered CI, claims, then dies three years later. Total paid across both events:
explanation: 25% of S$400,000 = S$100,000 on CI (rider ends), leaving S$300,000 for death. Total = S$400,000, the one basic pot, split. Acceleration never adds to the total.

question: A client wants a rider whose CI payout does NOT reduce his death benefit and can be sized at three times his basic sum assured. He needs:
options: An Additional Benefit CI rider
A 100% Acceleration Benefit CI rider
A 50% Acceleration Benefit CI rider
A Waiver of Premium rider
explanation: Only the Additional Benefit type pays a separate sum that leaves the basic intact AND can be a multiple of the basic SA. Acceleration reduces the death benefit and cannot exceed the basic SA.

question: A client insists on a S$500,000 CI rider attached to his S$150,000 whole life policy. Which rider type can accommodate this, and why?
options: Acceleration, because it pre-pays the sum assured
Additional Benefit, because it is a separate pot and can be a multiple of the basic
Either type, since CI riders have no size limit
Neither, because no rider may exceed S$200,000
explanation: Acceleration is drawn from the S$150,000 basic pot and cannot exceed it. Only Additional Benefit, being a separate pot, can be a multiple of the basic (here over 3x).

question: Which statement about the survival period on CI riders is correct?
options: It applies to both Acceleration and Additional types to prevent anti-selection
It applies only to the Acceleration type, because it pre-pays the death benefit
It applies only to the Additional Benefit type, to confirm genuine survival before paying a separate benefit
It replaces the waiting period for Multiple Pay plans
explanation: The survival period applies to the Additional Benefit type only. It guards the exit (proof of survival before an extra pot pays), whereas the waiting period guards the entry (anti-selection). Acceleration merely pre-pays the death benefit, so needs no survival test.

question: The waiting period at the start of a CI rider exists primarily to:
options: Confirm the insured survives the illness
Allow the cash value to build
Give the insurer time to set the premium
Prevent anti-selection by those who buy after suspecting illness
explanation: The waiting period guards entry: it stops someone buying the rider only once they suspect something is wrong. Diagnosis during it voids the policy with premiums refunded. Survival is a separate, exit-side concept.

question: A CI rider is issued on 1 March. The insured is diagnosed with a covered illness on 20 April, within the 90-day waiting period. The insurer will most likely:
options: Void the policy and refund premiums paid, without interest
Pay the full benefit
Pay half the benefit
Defer the claim until the waiting period ends, then pay
explanation: Diagnosis before or during the waiting period usually leads the insurer to void the policy and refund premiums without interest, since the waiting period is there to defeat exactly this.

question: Why does an Acceleration Benefit CI rider typically cover to about age 100, while an Additional Benefit CI rider caps around age 65?
options: Acceleration is cheaper so it can run longer
Acceleration is tied to lifelong whole life cover; Additional is a standalone-style top-up capped near retirement
MAS sets both ages by regulation
Additional benefits are only for children
explanation: Acceleration rides on the death benefit of a whole life policy, so it can run to ~100. Additional is priced like standalone CI, which insurers cap earlier (~65) as old-age claim risk climbs.

question: Two insurers define 'major cancer' with identical wording. A third covers an extra illness not on the standard list, using its own definition. Which is correct?
options: All three definitions must be identical by law
MAS writes every definition an insurer may use
The standard-list illnesses follow LIA standardised definitions; illnesses beyond the list are defined by each insurer
Only the SCI may approve any CI definition
explanation: The LIA standardises definitions for the listed critical illnesses, giving consistency. For conditions beyond the standard list, each insurer sets its own definition.

question: How many critical illnesses are on the LIA standard list, and what was the significance of the number 30 in this context?
options: 30 illnesses; 37 was an old proposal
45 illnesses; 30 is the current minimum
37 illnesses; 30 is the number of insurers who adopted them
37 illnesses; 30 was the old maximum-conditions-per-plan cap, abolished in 2014
explanation: The standard list holds 37 illnesses. The figure 30 was the old cap on conditions per plan, abolished in the 2014 review, letting insurers cover any number.

question: A client survives a covered heart attack and claims on his CI plan. Eighteen months later he suffers an unrelated stroke and wants to claim again. This is possible only under a:
options: Multiple Pay CI plan
Standard single-claim CI rider
Severity-based CI plan for one illness
Waiver of Premium rider
explanation: Two different, unrelated illnesses needing separate claims require a Multiple Pay plan. Severity-based deals with one illness worsening through stages, not separate illnesses.

question: A plan pays 20% of the sum assured when an illness is at an early stage and the balance if it later becomes severe. This describes a:
options: Multiple Pay CI plan
Severity-based (Early Pay) CI plan
Additional Benefit CI rider
Payor Benefit rider
explanation: Paying partial amounts as ONE illness progresses through stages is severity-based (Early Pay). Multiple Pay is about separate claims for different illnesses.

question: Which of the following is NOT among the exclusions typically applied to a Critical Illness rider?
options: A congenital or inherited disorder
Wilful misuse of drugs or alcohol
A first diagnosis of end stage kidney failure meeting the policy definition
Infection by HIV
explanation: A first-incidence covered illness meeting the definition is what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. Congenital disorders, drug/alcohol misuse and HIV are all listed exclusions. Watch the word NOT.

question: Under a Payor Benefit rider on a child's policy, premiums are waived when:
options: The child is diagnosed with a critical illness
The policy reaches its surrender value
The child turns 18 regardless of the parent's status
The paying parent dies or becomes disabled before the child reaches the specified age
explanation: Payor Benefit triggers on the PAYER's death or disability, waiving premiums until the child reaches a set age (usually 21 or 25). It protects the plan against the parent being unable to pay.

question: A client with a S$150,000 term insurance policy wants an extra S$150,000 of cover for ten years. The most appropriate action is to:
options: Increase the sum assured of the existing term policy
Attach a term rider to the term policy
Attach a decreasing term rider
Add a guaranteed insurability option rider
explanation: A term rider cannot attach to a term policy, because you would simply raise the term policy's sum assured. Term riders exist to add temporary cover to a PERMANENT base.

question: A Family Income Benefit rider pays S$3,000/month until the end of a 20-year term. Client A dies at the start of year 3; Client B dies at the start of year 18. Which family receives more in total, and why?
options: B, because surviving longer earns more
A, because more years of income remain from the date of death
They receive the same total
B, because the monthly amount rises over time
explanation: Income runs from death to the fixed end date. A has ~18 years of payments left, B only ~3, so A's total is far larger. It is a decreasing rider: earlier death, larger total.

question: A client wants cover that shrinks each year to match his outstanding home loan, attached to his whole life policy. The best fit is a:
options: Level Term rider
Family Income Benefit rider
Decreasing Term rider
Additional Benefit CI rider
explanation: A decreasing term rider's falling sum assured tracks a shrinking mortgage, and it is cheaper than a standalone decreasing term policy. 'Shrinking need' points to decreasing.

question: A client with a GIO rider is diagnosed with diabetes, then reaches his next option date. What can he do?
options: Claim a lump sum for the diabetes diagnosis
Nothing; a diagnosis cancels all remaining options
Only renew, not increase, his existing cover
Buy the additional cover at that option date without a medical, despite now being diabetic
explanation: GIO lets him buy additional cover at set dates WITHOUT evidence of insurability, so his new diabetes does not block the purchase. That is exactly the value of locking in the right while healthy. GIO never pays a claim itself.

question: A client skips exercising his GIO option on the age-30 date. What is the effect on the age-35 option date?
options: The age-35 option remains available; skipping one date does not cancel later ones
All future options are forfeited
He must pay a penalty to keep the age-35 option
The maximum cover at 35 is halved
explanation: Exercising GIO is not compulsory. Skipping one option date lets that one lapse but does not affect the right to exercise on later dates.

question: A client dies of a heart attack (an illness, not an accident). His S$250,000 policy carries an Accidental Death Benefit rider at the usual amount. The insurer pays:
options: S$500,000 (double indemnity)
S$250,000 (the basic sum assured only)
Nothing, because ADB excludes natural death
explanation: ADB pays extra only on ACCIDENTAL death. A heart attack is death by illness, so only the basic S$250,000 is paid. The 'double indemnity' extra requires an accident by external, violent and visible means.

question: A manual worker loses the use of one hand in a workplace accident but survives. Which rider would pay a benefit for this?
options: Accidental Death Benefit (ADB) rider
Waiver of Premium rider only
Accidental Death & Dismemberment / Disablement (AD&D) rider
Guaranteed Insurability Option rider
explanation: AD&D extends ADB to pay a percentage of the rider sum assured for dismemberment or disablement even where the insured survives. Plain ADB pays only on accidental death.

question: A Hospital Cash rider pays S$200/day. A client is hospitalised for 10 days for a covered illness and his medical plan already reimburses the full bill. The Hospital Cash rider pays:
options: Nothing, because the bill was already covered
Only the deductible portion of the bill
Half of S$2,000, because of coordination of benefits
S$2,000, because it pays a fixed daily sum regardless of actual charges
explanation: Hospital Cash pays a fixed sum per day of confinement regardless of the actual bill or other cover. 10 days x S$200 = S$2,000, useful precisely to offset deductibles or lost income.

question: A Hospital Cash rider pays a daily benefit and carries a lifetime cap of 730 times that daily amount. The clearest way to understand 730 is:
options: Roughly two years' worth of daily benefit (365 x 2)
A random regulatory figure
The number of insurers offering the rider
The maximum age for the rider
explanation: 730 = 365 x 2, i.e. about two years of daily benefit. Understanding the cap as 'two years of days' makes it logical rather than arbitrary. Once total claims hit the cap, the rider terminates.

question: A TPD rider pays its benefit in five annual instalments. Once the first instalment starts, what happens to the other riders on the policy?
options: All other riders continue unchanged
All other riders terminate, except an Extended TPD (ETPD) rider
Only the CI rider continues
All riders including ETPD terminate immediately
explanation: The moment the first TPD instalment starts, all other riders terminate, with the sole exception of an Extended TPD (ETPD) rider. Without ETPD, the policy also ends on the last payment or at the expiry age, whichever is earlier.
